FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD baseline sample, US study, 21 sites, ages 9-10 years

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen time is outcome variable (dependent), not exposure. Analysis examines family typologies → movement behaviors (PA, screen time, sleep). No qualifying smartphone/SM exposure variable.

FT_IC3.evidence: Screen time is outcome, not exposure; no inferential test of SM ↔ non-SM outcome. No relevant SM exposure analysis.

FT_IC6.evidence: Original empirical study, not a preprint/duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: N/A—screen time is outcome, not exposure. FT-EC7 logic does not apply.
notes: Paper examines family environment predictors of children's movement behaviors. Screen time is a dependent variable, not an independent exposure. Does not meet FT-IC2 (no qualifying exposure) and therefore fails the inclusion chain.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD baseline sample, US cohort, 9-10 year-old children, 9,718 participants

FT_IC2.evidence: Screen media activity measured on weekday and weekend; separate continuous variables submitted to ML model as independent predictors. Applies B1 (separate coefficient for screen time variable in regression/ML framework).

FT_IC3.evidence: Machine learning framework with regression-based variable importance and partial dependence analysis. Penalized regression coefficients reported (Table 2: r, pFDR, VI). Meets B8/B9 requirement for traditional regression coefficients in ML context.

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports Spearman r, pFDR (p-value false discovery rate corrected), and variable importance (VI) with 95% confidence intervals for screen media activity coefficients.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD empirical analysis using machine learning to identify factors associated with fluid cognitive functioning. Screen media activity included as independent variable predicting cognitive outcome.

FT_IC6.evidence: Original published study, not a preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen media activity is independent variable predicting cognition (outcome), not a dependent variable. Does not meet FT-EC7 condition (a)—SM is not the outcome in all analyses.
notes: Kirlic et al. 2021. Machine learning study identifying modifiable factors for cognitive functioning. Screen media activity (weekday and weekend) included as predictor; demonstrates ML approach meets FT-IC3/IC4 with traditional regression coefficients and VI metrics.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD prospective cohort, baseline 2016-2018 (ages 9-10) and 1-year follow-up 2017-2019, 11,025 participants, US study, 21 sites

FT_IC2.evidence: Six qualifying screen time modalities measured at baseline as separate independent variables: social networking (aOR 1.62, 95% CI 1.18-2.22), texting (aOR 1.40, 95% CI 1.08-1.82), TV shows/movies (aOR 1.39, 95% CI 1.14-1.69), video games, video chat, videos (YouTube). Total screen time also included. Applies B1 (separate coefficients for each SM/screen modality).

FT_IC3.evidence: Logistic regression analyses with adjusted odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals comparing baseline screen time exposure to binge-eating disorder outcome at 1-year follow-up (Table 2).

FT_IC4.evidence: Table 2 reports unadjusted and adjusted odds ratios (OR/aOR) with 95% CI and p-values for all screen time exposures and binge-eating disorder outcome.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original ABCD prospective empirical analysis: baseline screen time measures (exposure) → 1-year binge-eating disorder outcome (DSM-5 criteria via KSADS-5).

FT_IC6.evidence: Original published study, not preprint or duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: Screen time is independent variable (predictor); binge-eating disorder is dependent variable (outcome). Does not meet FT-EC7 condition (a)—SM is not the outcome in analyses.
notes: Nagata et al. 2021. Prospective cohort study examining baseline screen time modalities (social networking, texting, TV/movies) as predictors of incident binge-eating disorder at 1-year follow-up. Multiple qualifying exposures with separate adjusted odds ratios reported.
